questions.q: What is the popular meaning of the name 'Hazarduari'?
questions.explanation: 'Hazarduari' literally means 'a thousand doors', which is why it is called the Palace of a Thousand Doors.

questions.q: In which Indian state is the Hazarduari Palace located?
questions.explanation: Hazarduari Palace stands in Murshidabad, in the state of West Bengal.

questions.q: In which town is the Hazarduari Palace situated?
questions.explanation: The palace is located in Murshidabad, the former capital of the Nawabs of Bengal.

questions.q: Hazarduari Palace was built for which rulers?
questions.explanation: The palace was built for the Nawabs of Bengal, who ruled from Murshidabad.

questions.q: In which century was the Hazarduari Palace built?
questions.explanation: Construction of the palace was carried out in the first half of the 19th century.

questions.q: What architectural style is the Hazarduari Palace most often described as?
questions.explanation: Hazarduari is built in an Italianate, European neoclassical style rather than a traditional Indian style.

questions.q: What is notable about many of the thousand doors of the palace?
options: They are all made of solid gold
Many of them are false doors
Each opens to a separate garden
questions.explanation: Of the thousand doors, many are false doors painted onto the walls, intended to confuse intruders.

questions.q: Hazarduari Palace today functions primarily as what?
questions.explanation: The palace now serves as a museum housing the collections of the Nawabs of Bengal.

questions.q: Murshidabad, where the palace stands, sits on the bank of which river?
questions.explanation: Murshidabad lies on the bank of the Bhagirathi River, a distributary of the Ganga.

questions.q: Which agency maintains the Hazarduari Palace museum today?
questions.explanation: The Hazarduari Palace Museum is maintained by the Archaeological Survey of India.

questions.q: Which Nawab of Bengal commissioned the construction of the Hazarduari Palace?
questions.explanation: The palace was built during the reign of Nawab Nazim Humayun Jah of Bengal.

questions.q: Which British architect/engineer designed the Hazarduari Palace?
questions.explanation: The palace was designed by Colonel Duncan Macleod of the Bengal Engineers.

questions.q: During roughly which years was the Hazarduari Palace constructed?
questions.explanation: Construction of the palace was carried out roughly between 1829 and 1837.

questions.q: Of the palace's roughly thousand doors, how many are commonly said to be false (not real openings)?
questions.explanation: The palace is popularly described as having about 1,000 doors, of which roughly 100 are false (blind) doors and about 900 are real, the false ones intended to confuse intruders.

questions.q: Which prominent feature stands directly opposite the Hazarduari Palace within its complex?
questions.explanation: The Nizamat Imambara, one of the largest imambaras in India, stands opposite the palace in the same complex.

questions.q: How many storeys does the Hazarduari Palace have?
questions.explanation: The palace is a three-storeyed building.

questions.q: The grand entrance of the palace is approached by a prominent flight of what?
questions.explanation: The palace is entered via a broad, imposing staircase leading up to its main portico.

questions.q: What kind of collection is the Hazarduari museum especially famous for?
options: Antique weapons, paintings and royal artefacts of the Nawabs
questions.explanation: The museum is renowned for its collection of antique weapons, paintings, manuscripts and other royal artefacts of the Nawabs.

questions.q: Murshidabad, the seat of the Hazarduari Palace, was named after which Nawab?
questions.explanation: The city was named after Murshid Quli Khan, the first Nawab of Bengal to make it his capital.

questions.q: Which major 1757 battle, decided near Murshidabad, marked the rise of British power in Bengal?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey (1757) near Murshidabad was a turning point that established British East India Company dominance in Bengal.

questions.q: By what earlier local name was the Hazarduari Palace also known?
questions.explanation: Before it came to be called Hazarduari, the building was known locally as the 'Bara Kothi' (the great mansion).

questions.q: Hazarduari Palace was originally built to serve mainly as what?
options: A durbar hall and venue for official business and meetings with the British
questions.explanation: It was built chiefly as a durbar (assembly) hall for official business and ceremonies, including dealings with British officials.

questions.q: Hazarduari Palace is famous for a particularly large and ornate example of which imported European fitting?
questions.explanation: The palace is noted for a huge ornate chandelier, said to be among the largest in the region, hung in its durbar hall.

questions.q: The Hazarduari complex is located within which fortified enclosure at Murshidabad?
questions.explanation: The palace lies within the Kila Nizamat (Nizamat Fort), the fortified compound of the Nawabs at Murshidabad.

questions.q: The Nizamat Imambara opposite the palace was built after an earlier wooden imambara was destroyed by what?
questions.explanation: The present Nizamat Imambara was rebuilt by Nawab Nazim Mansur Ali Khan after the earlier wooden imambara was destroyed by fire.

questions.q: The exterior facade of the Hazarduari Palace prominently features columns of which classical order?
questions.explanation: The palace facade is fronted by a row of large Doric columns, a hallmark of its neoclassical design.

questions.q: Approximately how many large, lavishly decorated rooms is the Hazarduari Palace said to contain?
questions.explanation: The palace is described as having about 114 large, richly decorated rooms, including a durbar hall, banqueting hall, library and galleries.

questions.q: Which Nawab Nazim, son of Humayun Jah, later carried out major works such as the Imambara in the Hazarduari complex?
questions.explanation: Nawab Nazim Feradun Jah, also known as Mansur Ali Khan, oversaw later works including the rebuilding of the Nizamat Imambara.

questions.q: The land and city of Murshidabad were historically part of which Mughal-era province (subah)?
questions.explanation: Murshidabad was the capital of the Mughal Subah of Bengal, which the Nawabs governed semi-independently.

questions.q: Which of these statements about Hazarduari Palace is accurate?
options: It was built by Emperor Akbar in the 1500s
It is a 19th-century palace whose museum is run by the ASI
It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site built entirely of marble
It was the residence of the Maratha Peshwas
questions.explanation: Hazarduari is a 19th-century palace of the Nawabs of Bengal, and its museum is maintained by the Archaeological Survey of India.
